default.water: Moderate; deep water, keep foliage dry
default.soil: Well-drained, fertile loam with organic matter · pH 6.0–6.8
tips: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost (late Jan–Feb for Zone 8b)
Bury stems deeply at transplant — up to the top 2 sets of leaves
Stake or cage all indeterminate varieties early, before plants need it
Remove suckers for 1–2 leader training; more leaders = more fruit but smaller
Switch to low-nitrogen fertilizer once flowering begins
Water deeply and consistently — irregular watering causes blossom end rot
harvest: Harvest when fully colored and slightly soft to gentle pressure
Twist gently or cut stem — don't pull or yank
Morning harvest preserves the best flavor
Green tomatoes at season's end ripen indoors in a cool, dark spot (not touching)
companions: ✅ Basil — improves flavor and repels aphids & whiteflies
✅ Marigolds — deter nematodes and pest insects
✅ Carrots — loosen soil around tomato roots
✅ Parsley — attracts beneficial predatory insects
❌ Fennel — inhibits tomato growth
❌ Brassicas (cabbage, broccoli) — compete heavily for nutrients
❌ Corn — shares the corn earworm/tomato fruitworm pest
pests: Tomato hornworms — handpick or use Bt spray; check undersides of leaves
Aphids cluster on new growth — knock off with water or insecticidal soap
Blossom end rot = calcium/watering issue, not a disease — water consistently
Space plants and prune lower leaves for airflow to reduce early & late blight
default.safe: ⚠️ Leaves & stems are toxic to cats & dogs — fruits are safe

harvest: Determinate variety — most fruits ripen at once; excellent for canning runs
Harvest when fully red and slightly soft — typically 75–80 days
Dense, meaty flesh with few seeds — ideal for paste, sauce, and drying
Deep reddish-purple skin with dark green shoulders signals ripeness
Harvest at ~80 days — fruits feel firm with slight give; 8–10 oz each
Complex smoky-sweet flavor — best fresh-sliced or in small-batch premium sauce
Harvest when fully colored and easily detaches with a gentle pull
Check plants daily — cherry tomatoes ripen fast and crack if left too long
Most productive of all types; expect hundreds of fruits per plant per season
Large fruits up to 2 lbs — support heavy branches early to prevent snapping
Harvest at 85–95 days when fully red and just yielding to pressure
Meaty, low-seed flesh — the classic summer slicing tomato
Harvest elongated 3–5 oz fruits when fully deep red and firm — ~80–90 days
Sweeter, less acidic than Roma; thicker flesh and fewer seeds
The gold standard for pasta sauce, pizza sauce, and canning
Open-pollinated heirloom — save seeds for next year
Large oxheart-shaped fruits 6–8 oz — among the biggest paste tomatoes
Harvest at full red color around 80 days from transplant
Sweeter than most paste varieties — excellent fresh, roasted, or in sauce
Dense flesh cooks down beautifully; naturally thin sauce base — reduce to taste
Harvest when dark red-purple with green shoulders at 4–5 inches — ~80 days
Rich, sweet flavor with a natural salty edge and subtle smokiness
Excellent disease resistance: Fusarium, Verticillium, Late Blight, TMV
Striking dark color adds drama to salads and caprese; great for fresh eating
Earliest full-size slicer — harvest starts just 50–60 days after transplant
Globe-shaped fruits 4–5 oz; vibrant red with balanced sweet flavor
Outstanding disease resistance: Fusarium (R1&2), Verticillium, Nematodes + more
Check plants daily once ripening begins; harvest in cool mornings for best flavor
Medium-sized pink fruits 6–8 oz; harvest at ~75–80 days from transplant
Heat-tolerant heirloom known for setting fruit even in hot, humid summers
Mild, low-acid flavor — great for fresh eating; resists cracking
Large beefsteak fruits 1–2 lbs; harvest when green-yellow with a slight give — ~80–85 days
Unique fruity, sweet, complex flavor — one of the most distinctive heirlooms
Color stays green-yellow when ripe; don't wait for red — smell and feel to judge
Clusters of small red cherry fruits; harvest 65–75 days from transplant
Sweet, prolific producer — excellent for snacking and salads
Pick when fully colored and detaches easily with a gentle tug
Huge bicolored fruits 1–2 lbs with red and yellow streaks — ~80–90 days
Low-acid, sweet, fruity flavor with beautiful interior marbling
One of the most stunning heirlooms on the plate; great for showcasing at harvest
Rich, deep red-purple cherry fruits; harvest 64–70 days from transplant
Complex, rich flavor with a sweet-tart balance — one of the best cherry types
Very vigorous plant — prune suckers to keep manageable; stake firmly
Small elongated dark red-purple fruits; harvest at ~70–75 days
Sweet and rich with a hint of smokiness similar to Black Cherry
Striking appearance — black-red skin deepens to strawberry-red at blossom end
Large beefsteak fruits with striking blue-purple shoulders; harvest ~75–80 days
Mild, sweet flavor with excellent meaty texture; high in anthocyanins
Blue color on sun-exposed skin fades to red as fruit fully ripens
Small paste-type plum fruits with blue-purple skin; harvest ~78–85 days
Dense, meaty flesh with excellent flavor for its small size
Versatile paste tomato — great for roasting, sauce, or fresh eating
Long, slender plum fruits 4–6 oz; harvest at ~80–85 days from transplant
Excellent meaty flesh with very few seeds — outstanding for sauce and drying
Productive heirloom; fruits hold well on the vine without cracking
Large oxheart-shaped plum fruits 10–16 oz; harvest ~65–75 days
One of the earliest large paste tomatoes — meaty, nearly seedless flesh
Sweet, mild flavor; superb for thick sauce without long cooking times
Long pointed horn-shaped fruits 4–6 oz; harvest ~80–85 days
Very meaty with thick walls and small seed cavities — excellent for paste
Prolific producer; fruits stay firm and resist cracking through wet seasons
Long, tapered paste fruits 4–5 oz; harvest ~75–80 days
Polish heirloom with rich flavor and dense, dry flesh — ideal for sauce
Considered one of the finest paste tomatoes for flavor; rarely found in stores
Determinate plum tomato — most fruits ripen together for a canning harvest
Firm, meaty 4–6 oz fruits; harvest ~70–75 days from transplant
Crack-resistant and heat-tolerant; reliable production in Pacific Northwest summers
Beautiful orange-red elongated fruits with yellow stripes; harvest ~78–85 days
Excellent sweet flavor for a paste type — equally good fresh or in sauce
A cross between Antique Roman and Banana Legs — colorful addition to any garden
Elongated paste fruits with striking red-orange and yellow stripes; ~78–85 days
Meaty flesh with excellent balanced flavor — versatile for sauce or fresh eating
Beautiful presentation at harvest; fruits hold well and resist cracking

default.soil: Well-drained, fertile loam with organic matter · pH 6.0–6.8
tips: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost (late Jan–Feb for Zone 8b)
Pepper seeds germinate slowly — keep soil at 80–90°F; use a heat mat
Transplant with ideal transplants showing buds but no open flowers
Pinch first flowers on young plants to encourage bushier, more productive growth
Avoid excess nitrogen — it pushes foliage at the expense of fruit
Mulch around the base to retain moisture and regulate soil temperature
harvest: Most peppers can be harvested green or left to ripen to full color
Cut with scissors or pruners — pulling can snap branches
Regular harvesting encourages continuous fruit set throughout the season
Flavor deepens and sweetens as peppers ripen from green to red
companions: ✅ Basil — repels aphids and spider mites; improves flavor
✅ Marigolds — deter nematodes and aphids
✅ Carrots — loosen soil without competing heavily
✅ Spinach — low-growing ground cover that retains soil moisture
❌ Fennel — allelopathic; inhibits pepper growth
❌ Brassicas — compete for nutrients and attract shared pests
pests: Aphids — especially on new growth; knock off with water or insecticidal soap
Pepper hornworms — handpick; check leaf undersides for eggs
Bacterial leaf spot — avoid overhead watering; improve airflow
Blossom end rot — caused by inconsistent watering or calcium deficiency
default.safe: ⚠️ Capsaicin can irritate pets — hot varieties especially; wear gloves when handling

harvest: Harvest green at 3 inches for fresh heat, or leave to ripen red for sweeter, deeper flavor
First harvest 60–80 days from transplant; produces continuously until frost
Thick flesh ideal for stuffing, pickling, smoking (chipotle), or slicing onto nachos
Heat level can be tuned by watering — consistent moisture = milder; stress = hotter
Harvest at 60–75 days when slender and wrinkled green; don't wait for color change
About 1 in 10 peppers will surprise with unexpected heat (100–1,000 Scoville)
Best flash-fried or blistered in a hot pan; also excellent pickled or stir-fried
Thin skin cooks fast — harvest regularly to keep the plant producing all season
Harvest green at 1–4 inches for peak heat; ripens yellow, orange, then red
First harvest 75–90 days; 3–5× hotter than jalapeño (10,000–25,000 Scoville)
Thin skin means no need to peel — ideal for fresh salsas, hot sauces, and pico
Very prolific producer; a single plant yields 50+ peppers per season
Harvest green at full size (70–85 days) or leave to ripen to red, yellow, or orange
Ripe peppers are sweeter, higher in vitamin C, and more flavorful than green
Thick walls ideal for stuffing, roasting, or raw in salads
Support heavy-fruiting branches early to prevent snapping
Harvest when fully colored (orange, red, or chocolate) at 100–120 days
Extremely hot (100,000–350,000 Scoville) — wear gloves when harvesting and processing
Fruity, floral aroma distinguishes habanero heat from other hot peppers
Store fresh up to 2 weeks; excellent for hot sauce, drying, or freezing

tips: Start indoors in early April (cucumbers need warm soil — don't rush it)
Transplant when soil consistently reaches 65°F — usually late April/May in Zone 8b
Trellis vines to save ground space and improve airflow
Water at the base; wet foliage encourages powdery mildew
Feed every 2–3 weeks with balanced organic fertilizer after flowering begins
harvest: Harvest in the morning when fruits are firm and cool
Use scissors or a knife — pulling can damage vines
Most varieties ready 50–70 days after transplanting
Pick frequently — regular harvesting keeps plants productive
Select a variety below for size and timing specifics
companions: ✅ Marigolds — repel cucumber beetles and aphids
✅ Beans — fix nitrogen; improve soil for heavy feeders
✅ Dill (young) — attracts beneficial insects
✅ Sunflowers — provide trellis support and attract pollinators
❌ Sage — inhibits cucumber growth
❌ Potatoes — share blight susceptibility
pests: Most modern varieties resist Powdery Mildew & Cucumber Mosaic Virus
Watch for cucumber beetles and spider mites
Good airflow from trellising greatly reduces disease pressure

harvest: Harvest when 6–8 inches long and dark green
Pick every 2–3 days — frequent harvest keeps plant productive
Avoid letting fruits yellow; signals the plant to stop producing
Harvest at 3–6 inches for best pickling texture
Pick every 1–2 days at peak season — these set fruit fast
Smaller fruits (3 in) = crunchier whole pickles; larger (5–6 in) = spears
Harvest at 3–6 inches for pickling size
Sets fruit without pollination — expect prolific yields
Check every 2–3 days; small vines fruit heavily

Armenian.height: Vigorous vine to 4 feet — trellis recommended
harvest: Harvest at 6–8 inches for best flavor and texture
Can grow to 10+ inches — still edible but gets seedy
Thin skin — no peeling needed; great fresh or in salads
Harvest at 5–6 inches for peak eating quality
Thin, spineless skin — no peeling needed
Pick every 1–2 days; parthenocarpic variety sets fruit prolifically
Harvest at 3–6 inches depending on pickle style
Pick daily or every other day to extend season through late summer
Thin skin absorbs pickling brine exceptionally well

tips: Start indoors 4–6 weeks before last frost, or direct sow after frost danger passes
Thin seedlings to 12–18 inches apart for best air circulation
Pinch growing tips when 8–12 inches tall to encourage bushiness and more blooms
Deadhead spent flowers regularly to extend blooming season into fall
Avoid overhead watering — powdery mildew is the main enemy of zinnias
harvest: Cut flowers in the morning when temperatures are cool
Harvest when petals are fully open but before pollen shows at center
Cut long stems just above a leaf node — the plant will branch and rebloom
Change vase water every 2 days; zinnias can last 7–12 days as cut flowers
companions: ✅ Marigolds — fellow pest repellers; great color companions
✅ Cucumbers & squash — attract pollinators that benefit nearby vegetables
✅ Basil — aromatic deterrent to aphids; both love heat
✅ Cosmos — extend the season; similar care needs
❌ Fennel — inhibits growth of nearby plants including zinnias
pests: Powdery mildew — most common issue; space plants well and water at base
Spider mites in hot, dry weather — spray undersides of leaves with water
Japanese beetles — hand-pick in the morning when they are sluggish

harvest: Unique lime-green blooms — harvest at full petal extension for longest vase life
Green color intensifies in cooler weather; fades slightly in high heat
Excellent cut flower — holds well for 7–10 days in a vase

tips: Direct sow in place — sunflowers dislike transplanting (taproot tears easily)
Sow seeds 1 inch deep; thin to final spacing once 6 inches tall
Stake tall varieties (over 4 feet) in windy locations before heads form
Side-dress with low-nitrogen fertilizer at bud stage to encourage large heads
Plant in succession every 2–3 weeks for blooms from July through September
harvest: For cut flowers: harvest when outer petals just begin to open, before pollen drops
For seeds: wait until the back of the head turns brown and petals fully drop
Cover drying seed heads with a paper bag to protect from birds
Dry seed heads face-down in a warm, airy space for 1–2 weeks before storing
companions: ✅ Cucumbers — sunflowers provide a natural trellis and attract pollinators
✅ Squash & melons — shade the soil and reduce moisture loss
✅ Basil — aromatic pest deterrent planted at base
✅ Corn — classic Three Sisters companion; share structural support
❌ Potatoes — sunflowers may inhibit potato growth
❌ Pole beans — compete for light and structure
pests: Sunflower moths — inspect seed heads and remove larvae by hand
Aphids on new growth — knock off with a strong water spray
Birds love the seeds — net or bag heads if saving seed
Downy mildew in cool, wet springs — ensure good drainage and airflow

harvest: Multi-branching variety — expect many smaller 4–6 inch blooms in warm gold, red, and bronze
Cut stems early in the morning just as outer petals unfurl
Excellent for bouquets — each plant produces 10–20 stems through the season

default.sun: Full sun; afternoon shade OK in heat
default.soil: Well-drained, rich loam · pH 6.0–7.0; amend with compost at planting
tips: Plant tubers 4–6 inches deep with the eye (bud) facing up after last frost (late April in Zone 8b)
Do not water until green shoots appear — tubers rot easily in cold, wet soil
Pinch growing tips at 12 inches to encourage branching and more blooms
Stake medium and tall varieties at planting time before roots are established
In Zone 8b, tubers can overwinter in ground with 4–6 inches of mulch after frost kills tops
harvest: Cut stems in the morning; immediately place in cool water for best vase life
Harvest blooms when fully open — dahlias do not continue to open after cutting
Sear cut ends briefly over a flame or in boiling water to extend vase life to 5–7 days
Deadhead spent blooms regularly to keep plants producing into fall
companions: ✅ Marigolds — repel aphids and nematodes around tubers
✅ Basil — aromatic deterrent to aphids and thrips
✅ Cosmos — similar care; extends the seasonal display
✅ Zinnias — great heat-loving companions for full-sun borders
❌ Fennel — allelopathic; inhibits nearby plant growth
pests: Aphids and thrips — spray with insecticidal soap at first sign
Powdery mildew in late summer — improve airflow; avoid overhead watering
Earwigs damage petals — set beer traps or damp newspaper traps at night
Slugs on young shoots — apply iron phosphate bait at soil level
default.safe: ⚠️ Mildly irritating to cats and dogs — avoid ingestion (ASPCA)

harvest: Deep maroon-black decorative dahlia — one of the darkest available
Blooms are 3–4 inches across; long stems make excellent cut flowers
Harvest when petals are fully open; lasts 5–7 days in a vase
Seed-grown mix of vibrant reds, oranges, and purples with dark chocolate foliage
Single-flowered blooms 2–3 inches across — excellent for pollinators
Blooms freely without deadheading; harvest semi-open for best vase life

default.water: Moderate; reduce as vines die back
default.soil: Loose, well-drained, slightly acidic · pH 5.0–6.5; avoid freshly limed soil
default.frost: Foliage tender; tubers harden with age
tips: Plant certified disease-free seed potatoes cut into 2 oz pieces with at least one eye each
Allow cut pieces to cure for 1–2 days before planting to prevent rot
Plant in trenches 4 inches deep; hill soil up around stems as plants grow to prevent greening
Stop watering when foliage yellows and falls over — this signals tubers are mature
In Zone 8b: plant mid-March to mid-May; harvest July through September
harvest: New potatoes: harvest 2–3 weeks after flowering begins while skins are thin
Storage potatoes: wait until foliage fully dies back, then wait another 2 weeks
Cure storage potatoes at 50–60°F in high humidity for 1–2 weeks before storing
Store in a cool, dark, well-ventilated location; check regularly for rot
⚠️ Green skin or sprouts contain solanine — always discard green or sprouted potatoes
companions: ✅ Beans — fix nitrogen in soil; interplant between rows
✅ Horseradish — planted at corners of the bed, deters Colorado potato beetles
✅ Marigolds — planted nearby, repel nematodes and some insects
❌ Tomatoes — same family (Solanaceae); share blight and pest pressure
❌ Cucumbers — share susceptibility to blight; keep separated
❌ Fennel — inhibits growth of most vegetables including potatoes
❌ Sunflowers — may inhibit potato development
pests: Colorado potato beetle — hand-pick adults and egg clusters; use row covers on young plants
Late blight (Phytophthora) — most serious disease; plant resistant varieties; avoid overhead water
Wireworms — rotate crops; avoid planting in sod ground for 2+ years
Scab — keep soil pH at or below 6.0; avoid fresh manure at planting time
default.safe: ⚠️ TOXIC — green potato flesh, sprouts, and leaves contain solanine; always discard green potatoes

harvest: Classic yellow-fleshed all-purpose potato — ready in 70–90 days
Excellent flavor even as new potatoes (harvest ~60 days after planting)
Medium-sized tubers with thin skin — does not store as long as russets; use within 3 months
Vibrant red skin and pink-red flesh — retains color when cooked
Ready in 80–90 days; scab-resistant variety (great for Zone 8b wet springs)
Best for boiling and roasting; flesh stays firm and colorful

tips: Pinch blossoms until July 1st in first year — strengthens roots for better future yields
Remove runners to keep energy focused on fruit production
Day-neutral varieties produce regardless of day length — harvest June through October
Mulch crowns after first frost to protect through winter
Renovate beds annually after harvest — thin plants and refresh mulch
harvest: Harvest daily once berries turn fully red — check under leaves for hidden fruits
Refrigerate immediately in a single layer to prevent bruising
Everbearing season runs June through October in Zone 8b
Morning harvest gives the best flavor and longest shelf life
companions: ✅ Borage — deters aphids, attracts pollinators; said to improve flavor
✅ Thyme — repels worms; improves fruit flavor
✅ Marigolds — repel nematodes and aphids
❌ Fennel — allelopathic; inhibits strawberry growth
❌ Brassicas — compete for nutrients
pests: Birds — net plants when berries begin to color
Slugs — use iron phosphate bait at soil level
Albion resists Anthracnose, Powdery Mildew, and Verticillium Wilt
Phytophthora crown rot — ensure excellent drainage; avoid overwatering
default.safe: 🐱 🐶 Non-toxic to cats and dogs; fruit safe for humans

harvest: Day-neutral everbearing hybrid — large, firm, conical berries with exceptional sweetness
Harvest from June through October; pick daily as berries ripen fully red
Strong disease resistance: Anthracnose, Powdery Mildew, Verticillium Wilt
Performs well in both hot dry summers and cool maritime climates
